name: src/runtime layout (V1)
description: src/runtime/ holds the AgentRuntime seam: runtime.ts (interface + SpawnContext/ResumeContext/ParseContext/PrepareContext), registry.ts (AgentRegistry, BUILT_IN_RUNTIMES), claude-code.ts (spawn-per-turn, stream-json), sapling.ts (spawn-per-turn, NDJSON), codex.ts (one-shot, raw-text), declarative.ts (AgentConfig → AgentRuntime), version.ts (shared install-check probe), parsers/{jsonl-claude,stream-json,raw-text}.ts.

title: Use Zod 4 for AgentConfig validation (and future burrow.toml schemas)
rationale: SPEC §6 lists Zod as the validation choice for burrow.toml, AgentConfig, sandbox config, and message envelopes. Phase 4 needed AgentConfig to land — adding zod here gets the dep in place once and unblocks Phase 8's burrow.toml + secrets work without a second decision. Zod 4 was chosen because the runtime was new code (no v3 callers to migrate).

content: Declarative AgentConfig args support these tokens: {{prompt}}, {{workspace}}, {{run_id}}, {{burrow_id}}. {{prompt}} expands to the steering-prefixed prompt (pending messages then the user prompt) — same shape that promptDelivery=stdin writes to stdin. Unknown tokens are left literal so a malformed template surfaces as the agent's own error rather than a silent substitution.

Steering messages render to '[STEERING] (priority:

) ' when prefixed onto a prompt (sapling/codex/declarative) or wrapped as a Claude Code user-text envelope (claude-code). This single text shape is what burrow surfaces to every runtime, regardless of how the inbox is delivered (stdin-ndjson vs prompt prefix). Phase 5's inbox layer should reuse encodeInboxMessage rather than re-deriving the format.

name: registry-last-write-wins
description: AgentRegistry.register(input) accepts either a raw AgentRuntime or an AgentConfig object. AgentConfigs go through loadAgentConfig (Zod validation → ValidationError on shape failure) so malformed configs throw at registration time, not at first run. Same id later overrides earlier (SPEC §12.3 resolution order: built-ins → ~/.config/burrow/agents.toml → project burrow.toml). New ids extend.

content: AgentRuntime.prepareWorkspace receives the host workspace path, not the in-sandbox /workspace path. The bwrap/sandbox-exec wrapper bind-mounts ctx.workspacePath to /workspace at exec time, so any setup files (e.g. .claude/settings.local.json, .burrow/codex/<run>.txt) must be written to the host path before spawn.

name: agent-config-parse-result-shape
description: parseAgentConfig returns AgentConfigParseResult { ok: boolean; config?: AgentConfig; errors?: AgentConfigParseError[] } — NOT the zod safeParse {success, data, error} shape. agents validate command checks result.ok and reads result.config / result.errors accordingly.

content: AgentRuntime.installCheck() must populate InstallCheckResult.path with the resolved host binary path (Bun.which for bare names, the absolute path otherwise). burrow up reads this to populate SandboxProfile.toolchainPaths so the sandbox can locate and read the binary; without it, sandbox-exec/bwrap fails the bare-name lookup. Built-ins inherit via runVersionCheck; declarative agents resolve config.command the same way.

title: claude-code uses two distinct credential channels: ~/.claude (local, plan billing) vs ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (deploy, API billing)
rationale: claude-code's own auth precedence prefers ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env over the ~/.claude OAuth cache when both are present. Burrow leans on this: local sandboxes mount ~/.claude (burrow-980e) so dev runs bill against the user's Claude Pro/Max plan, while deployed targets (fly/docker/tarball, Phase 9) ship ANTHROPIC_API_KEY via [secrets] so unattended runs bill against the API tier. Don't forward the OAuth cache to remote/headless envs — the token is single-user-bound, plan rate limits aren't shaped for production agent loops, and refresh requires interactive /login which no remote machine has. No burrow.toml flag needed: the deploy environment dictates which channel is available, claude-code picks correctly. Document in SPEC §17.4 when burrow-980e lands.

name: agent-credential-forwarding
description: AgentRuntime.credentialPaths(): Promise<string[]> declares host paths to forward read-only into the sandbox (SPEC §17.4). burrow up calls credentialPaths() for every burrow.toml [[agents]] row whose runtime is registered, dedups in declaration order, and lands the result on SandboxProfile.readOnlyMounts. Per-agent opt-out via burrow.toml [[agents]] forwardCredentials = false (camelCase, schema-level z.boolean().optional() — undefined is treated as 'forward'). claude-code runtime returns existing [~/.claude, ~/.claude.json] paths, AND its prepareWorkspace copies host ~/.claude/.credentials.json to <workspace>/.claude/.credentials.json so the sandbox-resolved HOME=/workspace lookup finds it without a second /login. sapling/codex don't implement credentialPaths yet — they ship analogous caches later.

Initial host-credential forwarding for claude-code only handled the linux file path (~/.claude/.credentials.json), but on macOS Claude Code stores OAuth tokens in the macOS Keychain (security service: 'Claude Code-credentials') and the file doesn't exist on disk. The sandbox profile denies Keychain IPC, so claude inside the burrow reports 'Not logged in · Please run /login' even when the host is fully authenticated.
resolution: On darwin, prepareWorkspace shells out to 'security find-generic-password -s "Claude Code-credentials" -w' to read the OAuth blob from the Keychain, then writes it as <workspace>/.claude/.credentials.json (mode 0600). Claude Code's file fallback picks up the materialized creds. Pass plat + keychainReader as injected options on forwardClaudeHostCredentials() so tests can stub the Keychain without shelling out.

title: claude-code runtime spawns with --dangerously-skip-permissions
rationale: Burrow's bwrap/sandbox-exec sandbox is the actual enforcement boundary for any agent it hosts — Claude Code's interactive permission prompts are defense-in-depth designed for ungoverned hosts. Inside a non-interactive spawn the prompt has no human to respond and the agent deadlocks (observed: agent emitted 'The ml prime command needs your approval to run' text events and made no progress). The flag is added to both buildSpawnCommand and buildResumeCommand in src/runtime/claude-code.ts. Future agents/runtimes that exhibit similar interactive-confirmation behavior under burrow should bypass at spawn time for the same reason.

title: Per-burrow TMPDIR isolation for agents with UID-keyed shared tmp roots
rationale: claude-code's Bash tool stores command output under ${TMPDIR-/tmp}/claude-${uid}/<sanitized-cwd>/<session-id>/tasks/<task-id>.output and runs a startup-cleanup sweep across the entire UID-keyed root on every spawn. That root is shared with the host's claude-code session and every sibling burrow, so cleanup races produced intermittent <bash output unavailable: ... could not be read (EPERM)> failures (burrow-8452). The fix lives in the runtime: prepareWorkspace plants <workspace>/.burrow-tmp/ (with a '*' .gitignore so worktrees stay clean) and buildSpawnCommand/buildResumeCommand emit env: { TMPDIR: <in-sandbox workspace>/.burrow-tmp }. The in-sandbox workspace differs by sandbox primitive (bwrap remaps to /workspace; sandbox-exec keeps the host path), so claudeCodeBurrowTmpdir() branches on process.platform. No sandbox-profile change is needed — the workspace subpath is already file-write*. Apply the same pattern to any future runtime whose host tooling reaches into a shared ${TMPDIR-/tmp}/<tool>-${uid}/ root.
